data.title: Janus Henderson Research Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Portfolio management

Joshua Cummings, CFA

John Jordan

Investment environment

  • U.S. shares rose on positive corporate earnings news. Economic growth has been relatively stable despite a government shutdown, policy headwinds, and slower-than-expected employment growth.
  • The Federal Reserve (Fed) followed a September rate cut with two additional 25 basis-point (bp) rate cuts, in October and December, respectively.
  • Excitement around artificial intelligence (AI) was a strong driver of returns early in the quarter. The market began to broaden in the second half of October as concerns over valuations and potential investment returns led to declines in AI-related stocks. Nonetheless, stocks ended the year on a strong note.

Portfolio review

Netflix (NFLX) was a relative detractor despite delivering double-digit revenue and earnings increases, higher guidance, and a growing subscriber base and engagement. Despite these results, the stock sold off on concerns over the relative valuation. Investors also worried that the company’s planned acquisition of Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) could face regulatory hurdles or result in a bidding war. We continue to see long-term opportunity for Netflix given its scale, pricing power, and content library, which would be difficult for competitors to replicate. The company has continued to expand its customer base, especially internationally, and it has found more ways to monetize its platform through paid password sharing, price increases, and advertising. We continue to see a long runway for growth, supported by the company’s global, high-quality content and selective moves into live events and sports-adjacent programming.

Oracle (ORCL) was another relative detractor, amid volatility in AI-related stocks. Oracle has emerged as a leading player in the AI market, supported by its strong and growing position in the hyperscale market as well as close relationships with leading AI partners. Oracle’s cloud business has signed several multibillion-dollar contracts. This led to a large increase in remaining performance obligations (RPOs), which represent expected future revenues. Despite the company’s strong financial performance, the stock declined in the fourth quarter as investors grew more cautious about Oracle’s ability to fund the ambitious infrastructure buildout necessary to service these future contracts. Further, the market has concerns about the potential customer concentration within these RPOs. While we believe these concerns are real, we view the risk/reward as positive as an optionality sized position given the potential for Oracle to be a leader of the AI-capacity buildout, as well as its technological advantages and strategic business relationships.

Lam Research Corporation (LRCX) was a positive contribution in the information technology sector. The company manufactures specialized deposition and etching tools that are critical to producing the advanced semiconductors needed for AI. Lam has become a structural share gainer in wafer fabrication equipment (WFE) because of both its strong customer relationships and rising etch and deposition intensity across leading-edge foundry/logic, dynamic random-access memory (DRAM), and advanced packaging. Shares rose as investors grew more confident in Lam’s opportunity in the AI space, which has led to strong third-quarter results and expanded margins. The company also raised guidance as the management team indicated better-than-expected demand in the WFE market. We have continued to see long-term opportunity for Lam Research as a market leader in many of the technologies that enable AI.

Avidity Biosciences (RNA), another positive contributor, is developing next-generation therapies that target genetic drivers of rare neuromuscular diseases. We originally invested in Avidity because of our confidence in its management team, differentiated pipeline, and proprietary technology that generated compelling data in diseases like myotonic dystrophy that showed disease modification. Our conviction paid off when global pharmaceuticals company Novartis announced its acquisition of Avidity, leading to a jump in the stock price.

Manager outlook

Equity markets have continued to rally on strong corporate earnings and resilient U.S. economic growth. Investors have moved away from their worst-case fears about U.S. trade policy, which has boosted the performance of risk assets. We also have been encouraged by stable economic growth outside of the U.S., especially in Europe, which may offer opportunities for companies with international exposure. Many global central banks are now in easing mode, which has been another tailwind for equity market performance. While the extent of easing will depend on the interplay of economic growth and inflation, lower interest rates could reduce funding costs and provide renewed support for sectors such as residential construction.

While we continue to monitor economic developments and their impact on our companies, our approach aims to minimize broader macro and style factors while we focus instead on bottom-up stock selection. The foundation of our fundamentals-driven investment process is our exceptional team of analysts, who bring deep knowledge of their respective sectors. Our analyst team is constantly on the lookout for companies with strong or improving business models, exceptional management teams, and underappreciated competitive advantages. Through our time-tested, disciplined investment approach, we will continue to pursue our goal of long-term growth in capital.

data.title: Par Pacific Holdings: A Hidden Infrastructure Story
data.description:
Par Pacific Holdings (PARR) is now on a critical breakthrough, which is mainly driven by successful Billings factory integration, incoming Hawaii SAF project start and aggressive share buyback, which since 2024 was almost 9% of all outstanding shares. I believe that the market is still wrong about the company and values it as a small cap oil refinery, ignoring its geographical island economics advantage, stable logistics and retail trade segments, also we should add strategic transformation into sustainable energy infrastructure platform. I see asymmetrical opportunity and I believe that in 12 months the company is going to be worth around $57 per share.

Why Now?

I believe that the company gives us an ideal setup due to the latest Q3 2025 EPS shock, which the market was not really expecting. Even though in the headlines we saw exceptional $5.95 EPS, we should adjust this and take out one time $195.9 million SRE exemption. Let's call it normalised core EPS, which is around $2.10 per share, still above the consensus of $1.97. This just proves that base refining business is operating at peak effectiveness and does not depend on regulatory revenue.

Also, what I like and notice is float shrinkage effect. In 2024 the company bought around 9% of the shares, and new $250 million program might eliminate around 11.5%. The company has a quite high short interest level, and it becomes a powder keg to any positive surprise, as it would force traders to cover their positions in a limited liquidity environment. I believe that the main catalyst is going to be February 24th, Q4 results.

Business Model

What I like about PARR is that it is not just a simple speculative bet on oil refining margins. The company has that "island economics" model, which operates in isolated markets with very high barriers of entry to new players.

Strategic four factories network uses those regional dislocations. Hawaii refinery is the only one in the state, which services that captive tourism and military demand. Washington refinery is profiting from PNW region’s isolation, refining cheaper Canadian oil. Billings acquisition became the breakthrough point, which now allows to play on WCS-WTI price difference in PADD 4 region.

PARR pipeline and terminal network is giving us predictable revenue, which reminds me of MLP structure. This segment in Q3 2025 had record $37.3 EBITDA, which effectively covered the company’s interest costs and in my view completely derisked the company’s valuation. Also, retail, the company manages more than 120 fuel stations, so PARR is ensuring product offtake and gets additional margin in so-called "last mile".

Renewable energy is what I like a lot too. Hawaii SAF/RAD project is a critical catalyst in my strategy. Strategic JV with Mitsubishi and ENOES is not only giving them $100 million cash to finish the project, but also confirms PARR transformation into sustainable energy platform, which is the main driver now that I see that might re-rate the company after the earnings.

Financial Health

For investors that value safety, PARR currently seems to be strong on their balance, I might say strongest in their history. In September 2025, company had good liquidity of $735.2, which was a solid 14% growth compared with the quarter before. They had $159.1 million in cash reserves, which was also increased by SRE regulatory payout, which allows the company to decrease leverage ratio and maintain buyback programs.

It is very important to understand their operating effectiveness. Company’s DSO is just 18 days, which shows quite healthy cash flow management. Also, logistics and retail segments together generate around $240 million annual EBITDA. This stable cash flow, fully covers the company’s interest costs, so even at adverse refining cycle fluctuations, I think that PARR financial stability remains derisked. I believe that this creates quite attractive float shrinkage effect, which I mentioned earlier, and I expect that their new $250 million buyback program will eliminate additional 11.5% of shares, which mechanically will increase the value for shareholders.

As I mentioned before, I am waiting for February 14th, Q4 results. I will be watching for two factors: guidance for 2026 CAPEX and Hawaii SAF project timelines. This $90 million project is backed by strategic partners, and will be another breakthrough for the company as it will reorient it from pure hydrocarbon refiner to sustainable energy infrastructure player, which is quite trendy in our times.

For Risk Management And Portfolio Building

To better understand what really moves PARR’s share price and behaviour, we should try to conduct a statistical analysis. I have used daily stock returns from 2022 till today (16/02/2026). In short, my results showed that PARR is not a directly connected to oil, but its price is more connected to refining margins and economic cycle phases.

For me, the most notable was regression analysis, which shows that oil price changes explain only a small fraction of share price movement, I would even say quite insignificant. This confirms that refining companies’ profitability does not depend on oil prices, but more from refining margins. At the same time, gasoline price dynamics, which I used as a crack spread proxy, showed higher explanatory power, showing that margin expansion or compression is the main stock value fluctuation engine.

Jet fuel, yes, it is interesting that I used that, but my model while going through factors, stumbled upon this and shows regime sensitivity. Which just shows operating structure in Hawaii, where tourism and air transportation cycles could significantly affect fuel demand and regional margins.

Also, I have added overall market conditions, which are important to any stock. Even though average stock beta was 0.9, in critical fluctuations, it increases to 1.2, which is not that bad, as the company is simply acting as a cyclical industry stock, than defensive energy infrastructure asset. Volatility modeling showed high persistence, which means that when the market is in shock, the stock might act volatile and unpredictable for quite some time. Same, as beta, nothing to worry about as it is common for cyclical refining sector assets.

All in all, model shows that PARR stock behaviour is mostly formed by refining margins and demand cycles. So before buying, plan accordingly.

Risks

While their position is quite strong, and I cannot argue with that, it is worth looking at fundamental risks now. The biggest risk that I see is jurisdictional risk, which comes from Washington "Cap and Invest" carbon dioxide pricing policies. This system creates variable operational costs for Tacoma refinery. If the company fails to transfer these costs on consumers, this could affect and suppress refining margins. Also, I have noticed, TMX pipeline expansion could decrease Canadian oil (WCS) discount and that would affect Billings refinery commodity costs advantage.

I know that many will ask, what will happen if the oil price is going to decrease or fluctuate. As I mentioned in section above, it is not the main danger to the company’s profitability. I believe that is completely opposite as in 2026 guidance, the company already expects revenue drop due to commodity prices, but EPS is expected to be strong ($3.66-4.50), the profitability is more dependent on crack spreads (the difference between oil and product pricing).

The real risk, which I believe would affect any stock is recession. For PARR that would drastically reduce jet fuel and gasoline demand. Due to high operating leverage, if Hawaii margins dropped below $8/bbl, the company’s profitability would dissappear. Still, even in this black swan event, logistics and retail segments are generating stable $240 million annual EBITDA, which serves as quite good safety cushion for debt servicing.

Valuation

My DCF model shows that PARR value stands on moderate revenue growth, margin stabilisation and moderate free cash flow generation. As you can see in my mode, I expect that revenue in 5 years will grow, and I do believe this will happen, as the project we talked earlier, should be helping with this. EBITDA should grow fast too, mainly due to their operational effectiveness and scale economics, which lets EBIT and NOPAT metrics to go up. Based on my modeling, I do believe that the company is undervalued and we have room to run. Though, keep in mind, that I am investing into the company and I might be biased and this model is made bullish, overall, when playing with the model, I noticed that at bad scenario fair value is around $40-42, at best around $55-60. Due to this reason, I believe that the company is currently priced as the worst scenario and we might see re-rating.

Summary

Par Pacific Holdings is working in structurally favorable, isolated markets, where high barrier of entry let's them maintain their moat. Stable logistics and retail trading cash flows amortizes cyclicality and gives financial safety, which allows them to agressivelly decrease number of shares. SAF project together with strategical partners might change how the market looks at the company and transition them to energy infrastructure category. My statistical analysis confirms that profitability is dependent on margins and demand cycles, not just oil price. I believe that the right rating for the company is strong buy.

data.title: Light & Wonder: Playing The Margin Game
data.description:
I was going through my screener the other day and realized I haven’t looked at Light & Wonder (LNWO) in almost nine months. Time flies.

Somewhere along the way, this gaming giant delisted from Nasdaq, claiming they wanted to consolidate liquidity on the ASX (they previously had a dual listing).

But if you are or have been a Light & Wonder shareholder, you're used to big changes, aren't you?

Taking this hook to recap the story, the former Scientific Games reappeared on the market after selling its Lottery division to Brookfield and Sports Betting to Endeavor, reducing its long-term debt from a massive $8.6 billion in FY 2021 (Debt/EBITDA of ~14.5x) to $3.8 billion the following year.

With a lighter asset setup (Light & Wonder focused on Gaming, SciPlay, and iGaming), the market responded very positively, with the stock soaring triple digits since then.

When I first picked up coverage, this had been priced in for a while.

To be honest, I was analyzing it as an already established cash cow (predictable margins, stable revenue via long-term contracts, low Capex, and fat returns to shareholders through buybacks), something I did with other companies like Brightstar (BRSL) post-IGT, Inspired Entertainment (INSE) with its 'Operation Cash Cow', and others.

What probably wasn't fully priced in was the acquisition of Grover Gaming's charity gaming assets for $850 million in cash at the end of the first quarter.

Looking back, my 'Hold' rating worked well.

The stock has climbed close to 10% since the end of March last year, a touch below the S&P 22% in that same window.

Before getting into the numbers, one warning to holders. Steer clear of the OTC version.

As you probably already know, there is very low liquidity in this market. The company has made it very clear that you can do a 1:1 conversion by speaking directly with your broker.

Better Growth Beats Fast Growth

In May, I flagged that Light & Wonder was likely overearning its premium, given that growth was already slowing.

And you can see this very clearly.

While revenue grew ~3% to $841 million, adjusted EPS reached $1.81, about 35% higher than in the same period last year.

But if, like me, you're not a big fan of adjusted numbers, accounting EBITDA also grew in double digits to $322 million—the best reading in at least two years.

Operating leverage explains part of it, but what’s really lifting Light & Wonder’s profitability is the shift in revenue mix.

To see that clearly, we need to break down the segments.

Gaming—primarily VGT operations and charity gaming with Grover—for example, had sales of $558 million (the largest chunk of total revenue with a 66.3% share) and increased 4% year-over-year.

This segment delivered a 500 bp margin bump compared to the same quarter last year.

Taking a closer look here, what grew the most were VGT operations. To give you an idea, installed capacity jumped 42% compared to last year to an installed base of 47,240 in the United States and Canada.

Grover alone contributed 11,250 of the increase, implying that normalized growth sits around 8.5%.

Beyond the potential lift from Grover’s incremental capacity, the margin bump likely comes from the mix, with about 52% of machines sitting in the high-margin bucket.

iGaming—another high-margin segment—saw revenues growing 16% in the same period, increasing its relative share from 9% to 10.2%. It has an adjusted EBITDA margin of 40% (improving 800 bp compared to last year).

As I told you in the Inspired write-up, iGaming is much more profitable than sports betting in the states where it's permitted in the United States (Connecticut, Delaware, Michigan, New Jersey, Pennsylvania, Rhode Island, and West Virginia).

These are the most mature iGaming markets in the U.S., and combined GGR there is already around $7 billion, nearly five times bigger than sports betting. The seven states with legalized iGaming already generate around $8.4 billion in GGR, which is more than half of sports betting's $14.6 billion, despite sports betting being legal in 32 states.

According to AGA data, for example, GGR grew 28.3% to $9.7 billion (above sports betting and brick-and-mortar casinos in the same period).

What we're seeing at Light & Wonder is more high-margin recurring revenue coming from operators primarily seeking first-party content.

The only segment that posted a sales decline was SciPlay, but even there margins improved by 400 bp. Since it’s the lowest-margin segment at 36%, the revenue dip actually ended up helping overall profitability a bit.

Here, it seems to me that they are sacrificing some growth for monetization, achieving low to mid-single-digit ARPDAU gains.

Debt Still a Point of Concern

Besides the double-digit drop in international slot sales (churn in Australia/New Zealand and poor timing in Asia and the UK), for me, what weighs most heavily is the debt.

In the last quarter, they refinanced it by reducing the interest rate. Currently, it stands at $4.89 billion and, based on the TTM EBITDA ($1.1 billion), leverage is at ~4.4x.

If you consider that the bottom line is structurally expanding due to the mix, this could fall to ~3.5x without deleveraging, just due to EBITDA expansion.

So, even though it's a point of attention, it's not such a big problem.

Currently, they are being more aggressive in share buybacks.

In FY 2025 alone, they returned $661 million to shareholders and will likely continue to do so through more debt.

For the sake of our modeling, let's imagine a possible scenario here.

If we assume revenue of $3.34 billion (in line with Wall St. estimates), an EBITDA margin between 35% and 40% ($1.16 billion to $1.33 billion), and an EBITDA-to-OCF conversion rate of ~65%, we would arrive at a midpoint OCF of $809.2 million.

Capex should be around 17% of total revenue ($567.8 million), likely leaving us with a FCF of $241.4 million.

Modeling Shareholder Yield

Light & Wonder still has $735 million authorized for repurchases under the current program and intends to use debt to carry it out.

Assuming they utilize the entire amount by the end of FY 2026 and the average repurchase price is approximately $95 per share, they could repurchase and retire 7.73 million shares.

We’d be looking at the share count dropping from 83 million to 75.2 million, close to a 9.4% buyback yield.

What To Expect From The Upcoming Earnings

Looking back, Light & Wonder doesn't seem to be positively surprising Wall St. with its three consecutive quarters of double-missing expectations.

This time, they're targeting $914.8 million in revenue and an EPS of $2.11 ($1.53 normalized).

For them to achieve this, revenue would simply have to accelerate from low single-digit growth to 14.7% in just one quarter without any clear catalyst.

And notice, I'm not saying there aren't catalysts for Light & Wonder, but I'm specifically talking about revenue since this is a case of bottom-line growth from a healthier mid-market.

This was my main point in this write-up.

Now, for EPS, the gap seems to be a little smaller.

Assuming 83 million shares, the normalized EPS of $1.53 would be a net profit of $126.9 million. Basically, a growth of ~17.5% year-over-year, quite possible given what they've been reporting from here.

Since AGA continues to report iGaming GGR growth consistently close to 30% through November, I believe they would continue to see a greater influx of high-margin revenue coming from here, which would boost the bottom line and bring them closer to Wall Street expectations.

But look, I definitely don't see a double-beat. It's either a beat in EPS only or another double-miss since they are extremely optimistic about revenue.

-20% Last Month: What to Do?

If we take peers like Evolution (EVVTY) and Aristocrat (ARLUF), we would arrive at an average multiple of ~9x EBITDA for B2B content companies for iGaming/VGT.

Using the current share count (83 million), net debt of $4.7 billion (including the current portion of LT Debt), and an EBITDA range I previously modeled at $1.16 to $1.33 billion, we would arrive at a price target between $71 and $90 per share.

My sense is that the stock should sit closer to the high end of that range, given how much iGaming is helping lift EBITDA margins.

Looking at the long-term adjusted EBITDA target of $2 billion and assuming they actually achieve it by FY 2028, assuming a Net Debt/EBITDA ratio of ~3.5x ($7 billion net debt) and ~70 to 75 million shares outstanding, we would arrive at a price target of $120 already discounting it to present value.

Of course, this only works if execution stays tight and you’re willing to give it three years.

In the meantime, I see the stock hovering in the $90 to $100 range, with roughly 20% upside over that period if they keep marching toward the targets.

I'll maintain my 'Hold' rating here, targeting some bottom of the stock below $90 or $95 if you're aiming for the medium term (at least 2028).

What Would Make Me Change My Mind

As I showed you, my long-term price target of $120 million is based on the $2 billion adjusted EBITDA target for FY 2028.

Basically, anything that would make me change my rating to 'Sell' here would prevent them from reaching this target.

However, I have to say that since the setup is giving an upside of ~20% in three years (that is, we still have some safety margin, just not enough for me), there are more circumstances that would make me bullish than bearish here.

If the stock falls to ~$90, our upside here would increase to above 30% and justify the 'Buy'.

Another re-rating scenario would be iGaming revenue accelerating even further (I'd say above 20%) for several consecutive quarters, SciPlay continuing to improve margins and starting to grow.

For a bearish scenario, I see margins falling or stagnating, regulation weighing on traders within the United States, and more buybacks being financed with debt.

To me, it's a good story overall, but the timing is off.

That being said, if you are interested in the stock and want to follow it, monitor:

  1. The growth of iGaming and the margins of this segment (this is where the re-rating narrative lies, and if it weakens, this will be the link).
  2. Whether installed capacity is holding steady or not, excluding Grover's effects helps to better understand organic growth.
  3. If EBITDA increases throughout the re-rating and bottom-line expansion, the Net Debt/EBITDA ratio will decrease, improving the company's risk profile.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Titan Machinery: Management Is Handling This Downturn Well
data.description:
If we focus purely on fundamentals, I would say that we could all agree that things have not been going particularly well for shareholders of Titan Machinery (TITN) as of late. In my last article about the company, published in August of 2025, I acknowledged that the business was facing significant revenue, profit, and cash flow declines. The end markets for the company, especially the agricultural market, proved to be challenging for the business. But even with that weakness, I found myself impressed with certain changes that management was making. This included reducing inventory levels in order to boost near term cash flow. And while the stock was expensive, if we assume that it will eventually return to more normal levels of activity, then shares would look downright cheap. This caused me to take a more neutral stance as opposed to a bearish one. Since then, however, we have seen a bit more downside. The stock is down 2.9% while the S&P 500 is up 7.4%.

When it comes to certain financial metrics, management is certainly delivering. But sadly, I see this more as noise at a time when market conditions are likely to worsen from here. I am particularly concerned about agriculture, which accounts for the vast majority of the company's revenue. 2026 is unlikely to be a great time for the market. And when we consider how expensive the stock is on a forward basis, it's difficult not to be bearish. For now, I am going to maintain my neutral stance on the company. But in all honesty, it wouldn't take much from here to downgrade it.

Time to tread cautiously

Lately, things have not been going well for shareholders of Titan Machinery. We need look no further than the most recent quarter for the company. This is the third quarter of the firm's 2026 fiscal year. According to management, revenue amounted to $644.5 million. That's a decline from the $679.8 million reported a year earlier. Almost all of this drop came from equipment sales, which plummeted from $495.1 million to $459.9 million. But to really understand what's going on here, we need to look at demand for its different end markets. Agriculture has historically accounted for the vast majority of the company's revenue. In fact, during that quarter, it was responsible for 65.3% of overall sales. The problem is that agriculture revenue has been falling through the floor. While it was $420.9 million in the third quarter, a year prior it was $482 million.

It may seem as though the agricultural space would be stable. After all, people need to eat. However, the fact of the matter is that the industry has been going through some significant challenges as of late. Management blamed the weakness that the company saw on lower demand stemming from a decline in total crop receipts. The good news is that while the company does not provide much in the way of detail here, data is abundant. During the 2025 calendar year, net farm income in the US was $154.5 billion. That was up from the $127.5 billion reported the previous year. But actual crop receipts decline from $244.9 billion to $238.1 billion. In fact, that marked the third consecutive year of drops, with the highest on record being the $282.7 billion achieved back in 2022.

The USDA has made very clear why crop receipts have been falling. Some of this has to do with rising production that has pushed prices lower. But it's not just the revenue that matters. Higher costs have also been a problem. This stems in part from weather related issues and disease. Interestingly, the past couple of years have been a good time for revenue when it comes to animals and products. But what's important make note of is that while overall net farm income did grow from 2024 to 2025, when we strip out government funding, things do look worse. Year over year, federal government direct farm program payments jumped from $10.1 billion to $30.5 billion. So the actual revenue situation is worse on an organic basis.

There is some good news and there is some bad news when it comes to the 2026 calendar year. The bad news is that net farm income is expected to drop to $153.4 billion. This is in spite of the fact that government funding is going to rise further to $44.3 billion. However, when it comes to crops, cash receipts are expected to inch up to $240.8 billion. That's not a huge movement, but it's better than a continuous decline. Corn should be a positive, with cash receipts forecasted to climb from $61.6 billion to $63.7 billion. This is because of higher quantities sold. We should also see some improvement when it comes to fruits and nuts, as well as vegetables and melons. Higher prices should affect the latter of these, though for the former we don't really have an understanding of why there will be an increase.

Construction revenue for the company has worsened also. It dropped from $85.3 million to $76.7 million year over year. Equipment demand is weakening. Changes in government funding, and broader economic conditions like a weakening consumer and high interest rates that have made some construction activities prohibitively expensive, can be blamed for this. Fortunately, there was some strength. In Europe, for instance, revenue for the company skyrocketed from $62.4 million to $117 million. Strong stimulus programs in Romania proved incredibly instrumental here. But alas, that was not enough to offset the aforementioned weaknesses and a decline in revenue coming from Australia, with revenue they are plummeting 40.4% after the company saw a slowdown in demand. This weakness on the demand side was because prior years had seen stronger than normal revenue because of significant backlog expansion that the business ultimately worked through.

Revenue for the company certainly fell. But at least net profits inched up from $1.7 million to $2 million. The company benefited from an increase in its gross profit margin, with a firm attributing this mostly to the achievement of annual manufacturer incentive programs. Other profitability metrics did improve also. Operating cash flow went from negative $8.8 million to positive $34 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $9 million to $10.9 million. And finally, EBITDA for the company expanded from $14.7 million to $17.2 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first nine months of the 2026 fiscal year compared to the same time a year earlier. In this case, we saw once again a mixed picture. But for the most part, it was negative. Sadly, investors should expect this to be the new normal for a time. When it comes to the 2026 fiscal year in its entirety, management warned that agriculture revenue would drop by between 15% and 20%. Construction revenue would drop by 5% to 10%. And for Australia, revenue would decline by between 20% and 25%. Even though the aforementioned stimulus in Europe is expected to push sales there up by between 35% and 40%, the business is still expected to generate a significant net loss for the year.

If we do project out results for the company based on what we have seen for the first nine months, we would get adjusted operating cash flow of only $3.5 million and EBITDA of $4.1 million. In the chart above, you can see what this means for the valuation of the company compared to how it's valued using results from prior fiscal years. Normally, this would make me take a bearish stance. However, I am optimistic because management seems to be handling these issues in an intelligent way. Primarily, management is reducing overall inventories in order to reduce floorplan obligations. In the third quarter of 2025, for instance, the company had inventories of $1.41 billion. By the third quarter of the 2026 fiscal year, this had dropped to only $1.01 billion. Crucially, the company was able to reduce the amount of floor plan obligations at a similar rate, as evidenced by the fact that inventories grew from 1.35 times floor plan payables to 1.37 times. This means that management is intentionally paying down these obligations as inventory is sold off during these tough times. This is a wise move that makes a lot more sense than allocating capital and other ways such as through acquisition activities or returning capital to shareholders. This means that the company is managing this decline in a manner that reduces the overall risk of a balance sheet blow up.

Takeaway

Near term, I would make the case that the fundamental picture for Titan Machinery is likely to worsen. 2026 probably won't be all that great for the firm. Having said that, the company has exercised a principled approach to dealing with this crisis. At some point, the agricultural space will recover. And when it does, it's not unthinkable that shares could rise quite nicely. After all, relative to the kind of financial performance that the business achieved in 2024, shares are incredibly cheap right now. For those who don't mind waiting and don't mind the prospect of short term downside, this is definitely a candidate to consider. But because risk here is involved, I believe that keeping the company rated a ‘hold’ is the most appropriate course of action.

data.title: Valero Energy: Still Pumping Cash, But The Valuation Looks Bloated
data.description:
Thesis

Valero Energy's (VLO) share price is at an all-time high. This coincides with a rebound across the entire refining segment as the supply and demand balance appears favorable in the near and intermediate terms.

This outlook is constructive for refiners but particularly so for VLO. The company historically has the lowest operating costs per barrel versus the competition. However, the current valuation may be overly optimistic.

While still industry-leading, VLO's valuation is the highest it has been in years and several turns above the company's 25-year average. This valuation does not coincide with equivalent profit margins. In fact, current profit margins are only “average” by historical contexts.

Normally, this would make a logical rationale to start trimming down on a position in a cyclical business. I have elected to rate VLO as a HOLD due to its competitive advantage in operating costs. Simply, no matter how bad the cycle gets, if anyone is making money, it will be Valero.

State Of The Refining Industry

Crack spreads were exceptionally strong in October and November on the back of low inventories across both gasoline and distillate fuels. Operators clearly responded by running their units harder than the seasonal norm. Specifically, VLO completed the year with 98% utilization in the fourth quarter.

The rest of the big three also ran well, with Marathon Petroleum (MPC) and Phillips 66 (PSX) registering utilizations of 94% and 99%, respectively. As a result, total refined product inventories recovered quite well.

As shown in the figure above, gasoline inventories have recovered from a five-year low to a five-year high in just a few months. VLO seemed a bit surprised at the magnitude of this buildup, as the company also noted the abnormally large inventory build in the recent earnings call.

Gary Simmons—VLO Chief Operating Officer

Certainly, during November and December, we saw a fairly significant build in total light product inventory. It followed typical seasonal patterns, but the magnitude of the build was much larger than what we typically see.

On the distillate front, an extreme inventory build was avoided thanks to an abnormally cold stretch in the Northeast, as well as the effects of winter storm Fern. Now, inventory sits at more typical levels but is still elevated on a year-over-year comparison.

While inventories are an important aspect when determining refining margins, available capacity can also play a large role in determining the direction of future crack spreads. After a year of industry contraction in 2025, VLO now forecasts a year of balance between supply and demand growth.

Gary Simmons—VLO Chief Operating Officer

Our numbers would indicate about 400,000 barrels a day of net capacity additions. We're showing about 500,000 barrels a day of total light product demand growth.

Most of this growth is expected to be in Asia, while the most notable closure will be VLO's own Benicia refinery. Fortunately, the additional supply in the Pacific basin is unlikely to impact many of VLO's key customers. As shown below, the company's heavy Gulf Coast presence keeps the majority of its export business serving the Atlantic basin as well as western South America.

Cumulatively, the outlook for refining appears stable. While domestic inventories are well stocked, it required major producers to run nearly flat out to get there. As we enter the seasonal maintenance period, utilization rates are likely to decline, providing some needed balance to domestic markets.

The timing of international growth is also a key factor. The entrance of new Asian supply is expected to be weighted toward the back half of 2026. The late start combined with generally lengthy ramp times makes it unlikely that the anticipated 400,000 barrels of new capacity will be fully online this year. Thus, global demand should be expected to remain tight in 2026.

Valuation

While the outlook for refining is constructive, it does not appear to be outside the normal fluctuations in a cyclical market. This appears to be in contrast to VLO trading at a historic high.

As shown below, the share price has now exceeded the previous peak in early 2024. At that time margins were quite robust, briefly exceeding 10%. Fast forward two years later, and margins are less than half of that value and more in line with the pre-COVID operating environment.

To provide a wider and more historical data set, I graphed VLO's operating margin over the last 25 years. As we can see, VLO has generated a positive operating margin in every year except in the wake of the 2008 financial crisis and the COVID-19 shutdown. However, today's current market is producing slightly below the 25-year average in terms of overall profitability.

Let's match this up with the company's current valuation. While VLO remains the cheapest option of the big three, the company's valuation is at a multiyear high.

Going back to the 25-year trend, we can again see how VLO currently stacks up against historical refining markets. In this context, VLO now trades 1.5-2x above the company's historical average.

My last point will be free cash flow yield. VLO returned $4 billion to its shareholders out of $6 billion in operating cash flow. After investing $1.8B of capital to sustain the business, investors were rewarded with nearly all of the cash generated by VLO. At the current share price, this translates into an FCF yield of around 7%. This is somewhat of a middling number.

Cumulatively, we have a 7% FCF yield with below-average operating margins. After factoring in the elevated valuation, it is fair to conclude that the current valuation is not aligning with current levels of profitability.

Why A Hold Rating?

If the company is overvalued, what is the appropriate rating? If you picked up shares earlier this year, then you are up quite significantly. Any investor should at least consider trimming your position to lock in profits. No one ever lost money taking profits.

However, even with the bloated valuation, I still view VLO as worthy of a core position in any energy portfolio. The rationale here centers around the company's leading cost position.

In the chart below we can see all major public refiners' operating costs per barrel. I often cite this metric when evaluating these companies, and you can see why when we circle back to operating margin. In an industry with an average operating margin in the ballpark of 4%, every penny per barrel matters.

VLO has consistently been the low-cost provider among any operator. This provides for a wide-moat competitive advantage and ensures profitability across the cycle. No matter how bad the cycle gets, VLO will be in the best shape. It also means VLO's margins will be the largest at the top of the cycle.

This is a rare attribute and is worthy of a premium. For this reason, I feel VLO remains worthy of a HOLD, although its share price may experience a correction in the near term.

Risks

As I discussed earlier, inventories are quite high. Gasoline, the major driver of profits in the summer driving season, is at a five-year high. The current thought process is that high industry utilization rates are unlikely to be sustained, leading to a correction in inventories.

This is certainly plausible, but the extent of that correction will determine if refiners will have a less lucrative summer driving season. As shown below, the current expectation is for VLO to generate the bulk of its yearly profits in Q2 and Q3. Missing expectations here would certainly push VLO into fully overvalued territory.

Investor Key Takeaways

1. Q1's earnings potential is lower following a rapid recovery in inventories. Crack spreads are notably down midway through the quarter.

2. Capacity growth appears muted and well balanced with demand growth in 2026. Volumes should be expected to arrive in late 2026 and early 2027.

3. Current profit margins are below the company's 25-year average.

4. The current valuation is also 1.5-2.0x above the historical EV:EBITDA level.

5. I rate VLO as a HOLD due to its competitive advantage on cost versus peers.

6. Key risks would be elevated gasoline inventories hurting margins in the upcoming driving season.

data.title: ZIM Integrated: Buying The 25% Shipper Arbitrage
data.description:
ZIM Integrated Shipping Services Ltd. (ZIM) is an Israeli-based maritime shipper that just announced an acquisition deal for $35 per share. The share price remains below $29 as I write this, and so we have a gap to evaluate and why the market might be discounting it. I will also give an overview of the options for ZIM at this moment.

Summary of Previous Thesis

When I last wrote about ZIM in October, shares were trading under $14. A potential acquisition was still mostly rumor, with little said by the board of directors. I therefore focused on the fundamentals and considered a buyout as more of a secondary benefit.

As shippers frequently pay their earnings to shareholders as dividends, ZIM's share price correlated very strongly with the Containerized Freight Index, which aggregates spot rates in maritime shipping. As rates had been lower than the peaks of 2021 and 2022 (caused by COVID-related supply chain disruptions), ZIM was lower, and I believe investors were getting a profitable shipper in its low cycle and concluded:

Country-specific stocks might seem like plays to be made on the news, but ZIM remains a global shipper, with infrequent dividends that nevertheless pay richly when they yield. The possibility of a much higher exit as better offers are sought gives shareholders another way to realize upside. For those reasons, I maintain my Buy rating.

Details of the Acquisition

The counterparty that is acquiring ZIM is Hapag-Lloyd Aktiengesellschaft (HPGLY), another maritime shipper. Key details include:

  • Enhanced capabilities with a large, modern fleet of over 400 vessels.
  • FIMI's newly formed Israeli liner company, "New ZIM", with a fleet of 16 vessels and a focus on directly connecting Israel to major ports in the EU, US, Mediterranean Sea, and Black Sea, will have access to Hapag-Lloyd's Gemini network.
  • Partnership with FIMI to assume Special State Share obligations with a clear objective to provide a continued secure liner shipping service to Israel.
  • "New ZIM" will have commercial support from Hapag-Lloyd.
  • Hapag-Lloyd expressed its intention to maintain a significant business presence in Israel, providing for the long-term employment of ZIM employees.
  • Acquisition price of $35 cash per share, total deal value of $4.2B.
  • Shareholder and Israeli approval are still needed.

It may not be immediately clear why some of these details are important. For one, approval by the State of Israel is the major milestone before the deal closes. Let's review the Special State Share. The most recent Form 20-F states (pg. 36):

The State of Israel holds a Special State Share in us, which imposes certain limitations on our operating and managing activities and could negatively affect our business and the results of our operations. These limitations include, among other things, transferability restrictions on our share capital, restrictions on our ability to enter into certain merger transactions or undergo certain reorganizations, and restrictions on the composition of our Board of Directors and the nationality of our chief executive officer, among others.

To most eyes, this reads as a major risk factor, and certainly it is from their Risk Factor section of the annual report. This indicates that approval is needed both by regulatory authorities that control Israeli shipping, as well as based on Israel's ownership in the business. For example, the "new ZIM" component is a requirement in Israel of minimal vessel ownership, as most of ZIM's business is a leased fleet, not owned. The deal is therefore structured to make this an easy decision for Israeli officials.

This is also an all-cash deal, so the Special State Share being the one exception, ZIM shareholders are staring at a $35 payment for their ownership. For practical purposes, this changes the thesis. Where I looked at fundamentals and spot prices before, ZIM is now an investment in an acquisition target, no longer a shipping play.

Outlook and Valuation

If one looks at the market's response to this announcement, which occurred over the weekend, there is still some apparent value on the table.

Shares climbed to $29 Tuesday morning, then declined slightly to $28. The math here is simple: $7 is on the table. Held to $35, this suggests a 25% return on a deal that should close before 2027.

For folks interested in the options on ZIM (and I did purchase calls, too), a similar value discrepancy can be seen. January 2027 $29.16 calls trade just under $3.00, despite $5.84 in implicit intrinsic value. Here, the returns could be leveraged from 25% into a double!

Naturally, we might ask what risk factors could complicate gains in either position, beyond the formal ones of shareholder approval and regulatory.

One question we might ask is if Hapag-Lloyd will make good on its offer. Considering their history of M&A, I think they will. In the 2020s, they have already made the following acquisitions:

  • SM SAAM's terminal business (2023).
  • Deutsche Afrika-Linien container liner business (2022).
  • NileDutch (2021).

These deals all occurred at the agreed-upon prices, upon approval by the authorities. This shows that Hapag-Lloyd follows through and successfully structures the deals to satisfy the rules of the various countries involved.

Another complication comes from within ZIM itself. Namely, employees within the Histadrut labor union immediately went on strike after the announcement. The Times of Israel reported:

The Histadrut said it is in discussions with Zim’s management and the workers’ union to secure and protect employees’ jobs.

“We will not hesitate to use all the tools at our disposal, including shutting down the company, to protect workers and the public interest,” the Histadrut said in a statement.

While this isn't the first time M&A resulted in pushback from unions, this was not a complication with Hapag-Lloyd's previously cited deals. It remains to be seen how quickly ZIM's management and Hapag-Lloyd can reassure workers. This may reflect why the ZIM's share price fell short of $30 after the announcement.

Current holders of ZIM who are accustomed to dividends may see any suspended, pending the acquisition. Dividends were already lower because of depressed spot prices, with the most recent being paid in late 2025 at $0.31 per share. It's already not consistent every quarter, and the workers' strike may not complicate the deal, but it may make it harder to pay dividends in the meantime.

As the call options themselves are concerned, the Options Clearing Corporation makes adjustments to extant contracts when a merger or acquisition happens in order to make sure they are resolved. For example, options with expiries set after the acquisition date would need to be resolved sooner, since the stock will soon be delisted. Technically, the OCC has discretion in this, the details of which are found here (Chapter XXVIII). An important standard they mention for all-cash transactions:

When an underlying security is converted into a right to receive a fixed amount of cash, such as in a merger or a call or redemption of an entire class of index-linked securities, the Corporation shall adjust outstanding options and outstanding stock futures to replace such underlying interest with cash in an amount per share or unit equal to the conversion or redemption price.

The memo they released (PDF download) for the acquisition of Kellanova last December is a clear example of shareholders receiving a settled result in cash. As such, I think ZIM calls with strikes under $35 and held to the merger are likely to be resolved this way, which would benefit anyone who buys that future cash value (merger price less strike price) at a discount.

In summary, the risk factors are:

  • Approval of the State of Israel, on the regulatory and shareholder front.
  • Union strike.
  • Suspension of dividends until merger.
  • OCC's discretion in adjusting call options may seem profitable.

Conclusion

ZIM is an interesting security, with a clear M&A offer on the table, but a large discount is reflected in the market. Perhaps the market doesn't believe them, but I do. Risk factors acknowledged for what they are, I believe the deal will close, a nice arbitrage in a period consumed by bearishness and fear.

data.title: DTE Energy Has Promising Growth Prospects
data.description:
About five years ago, I recommended buying DTE Energy (DTE) for its attractive combination of earnings growth and a generous dividend. Since that article, the stock has offered a total return of 68% or 10.6% per year on average. This is certainly an attractive return for a low-risk stock, such as a utility. Since my initial article, I have frequently reiterated my bullish thesis on the stock. My last article was in late 2023, when I stated that DTE Energy had become too cheap to ignore. Indeed, since that article, the stock has offered a total return of 60% or an average annual return of 22%.

The big question is whether DTE Energy remains attractive or its shareholders should take their profits. It is also worth noting that the company reported its full-year results yesterday. In this article, I will analyze the latest earnings report and discuss whether the prospects of the utility remain attractive.

Business overview

DTE Energy is a leading energy provider, with a strong presence in Michigan and a history of 177 years. Its electricity segment is the most important, as it generates 68% of the total operating earnings of the company while the gas division generates 16% of the total operating earnings.

DTE Energy reported its full-year results yesterday. The earnings report was positive, as it revealed that the company finished the year with strong momentum.

Source: Investor Presentation

The electricity segment greatly benefited from rate hikes thanks to past investment projects. It was also assisted by warm summer weather and higher renewable energy earnings, which more than offset increased operating and maintenance costs. As a result, the electricity segment grew its operating earnings 10% in 2025.

A similar picture was evident in the gas segment, which experienced tailwinds from cold winter weather and rate hikes thanks to past investment projects. The gas segment grew its operating earnings 12% in 2025. DTE Energy also benefited from its strong performance in the production of renewable energy. Overall, the company grew its earnings per share 8%, from $6.83 in 2024 to $7.36 in 2025, thus exceeding the mid-point of its guidance of $7.09-$7.23 by 3%. Such an earnings beat may seem minor in other sectors but it is undoubtedly significant for a utility, as utilities are slow-growth stocks in general.

More importantly, DTE Energy has promising growth prospects ahead. The company recently negotiated its first hyper-scale data center contracts to provide 1.4 GW of load to the new data center of Oracle in Michigan. This project can create $300 million of annual benefits for the existing customers of the utility. In addition, DTE Energy has another 3 GW of projects in advanced discussions. Thanks to its strong business momentum, the company just raised the amount of its 5-year capital plan from $30.0 billion to $36.5 billion.

Source: Investor Presentation

This amount is 121% of the market capitalization of the stock and hence it is a testament to the promising growth potential of the company. In other words, DTE Energy plans to invest aggressively in its future growth.

Management has repeatedly provided guidance for 6%-8% growth of earnings per share over the long run. However, thanks to the exceptionally strong business momentum right now, management stated that the 3 GW projects can drive the annual growth of the bottom line above 8% until 2030. This is an outstanding growth rate for a utility.

Management also provided positive guidance for 2026.

Source: Investor Presentation

DTE Energy expects at least 10% growth of operating earnings in its major segments and thus it provided guidance for earnings per share of $7.59-$7.73 in 2026. This guidance may appear lackluster on the surface, as it corresponds to 3%-5% growth year-over-year. However, management stated that it is positioned to achieve the high end of the guidance thanks to tax credits.

It is also important to note that DTE Energy exceeded the high end of its guidance in 2025 and has exceeded its own guidance in 18 of the last 19 years. Therefore, its management has proved conservative in its guidance. In addition, DTE Energy has exceeded the analysts’ estimates in 6 of the last 7 quarters. Overall, the utility has good chances of meeting or exceeding its guidance in the upcoming years.

Analysts seem to agree on the promising growth potential of the company. They expect it to grow its earnings per share by 5% this year and by 7.5%-8.0% per year through 2030. Given the accelerated business momentum thanks to the boom of data centers and the statement of management that the annual growth of earnings may exceed the high end of 8.0% until 2030, one can reasonably expect DTE Energy to meet or exceed the analysts’ estimates in the upcoming years.

Valuation

DTE Energy is currently trading at a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 18.75x, which is equal to the 5-year average of the stock. However, this earnings multiple is lower than the median forward price-to-earnings ratio of 20.0x of the utility sector. It is also important to note that DTE Energy seems to have improved growth prospects right now vs. the last few years. To be sure, it is the first time in its history that the company issues a 5-year capital plan of $36.5 billion. Therefore, the stock is likely to deserve a premium compared to its 5-year average valuation level.

Even if DTE Energy does not deserve a premium valuation vs. its historical valuation, it is trading at a discount to the median valuation of the utility sector. It is also worth noting that the stock is trading at only 17.4 times its expected earnings in 2027 and 13.9 times its expected earnings in 2030. On the one hand, most investors will claim that such distant valuation levels are highly uncertain, as many things can go wrong over such long time horizons. On the other hand, as a utility, DTE Energy has a highly reliable and predictable business model and hence the analysts’ estimates carry much lower risk than the analysts’ estimates for stocks of other sectors. To cut a long story short, DTE Energy appears attractively valued for investors with a long-term perspective.

Risk

As mentioned above, the business risk of DTE Energy is minimal, given the reliable business model of the utility. Of course, mild weather during the summer or winter can take its toll on the results of the company in one year but it is highly unlikely to impact the results of the company over the long run.

Therefore, the primary risk factor for the shareholders of DTE Energy is the adverse scenario of a resurgence of inflation and interest rates in the upcoming years. In such a case, the earnings of the company are not likely to be affected but the valuation of the stock will probably incur a compression. Utilities are among the most sensitive stocks to interest rates, as investors buy these stocks primarily for their dividend yield. If inflation rises from its current 4-year low of 2.4% above 3.0%, it may lead the Fed to raise interest rates. In such a case, the current dividend yield of 3.2% of DTE Energy will become less attractive and hence the stock will probably come under pressure so that its dividend yield becomes aligned with the prevailing yields in the market.

On the bright side, inflation has cooled to a 4-year low lately and hence there are no signs of a resurgence of inflation above 3.0% anytime soon. Nevertheless, those who invest in utility stocks should be aware of this risk factor.

Final thoughts

DTE Energy has rallied 12% this year thanks to its accelerated business momentum but the stock remains attractive for investors with a long-term point of view. The stock appears reasonably valued and seems capable of delivering 8% average annual growth of earnings per share until at least 2030. If the 3.2% dividend yield is included in the picture, the stock has the potential to continue delivering a double-digit average annual total return in the upcoming years.

Stock markets, especially foreign markets, are volatile and can decline significantly in response to adverse issuer, political, regulatory, market, or economic developments. Fixed income investments entail interest rate risk (as interest rates rise bond prices usually fall), the risk of issuer default, issuer credit risk and inflation risk. Foreign securities are subject to interest rate, currency exchange rate, economic, and political risks. Changes in real estate values or economic downturns can have a significant negative effect on issuers in the real estate industry. Lower-quality bonds can be more volatile and have greater risk of default than higher-quality bonds. Value stocks can perform differently than other types of stocks and can continue to be undervalued by the market for long periods of time.

Market Review

U.S. stocks gained 2.66% in the fourth quarter, according to the S&P 500® index, extending a historically fast rebound that began in early April, but at a slower pace. The advance has been supported by strong corporate fundamentals, a resilient economy, an ongoing boom in spending on artificial intelligence and the U.S. Federal Reserve's first interest-rate reductions since December 2024. Amid this favorable backdrop for risk assets, the index closed the year just shy of its all-time high. Value stocks modestly outpaced their growth counterparts in Q4, while large-caps had only a slight advantage over small-caps, as investors' appetite for risk waned versus the prior three months.

Stocks entered October on an uptrend, but with the federal government in a shutdown that would last until November 12. Equity markets rose nonetheless, boosted by earnings momentum and a constructive expansionary backdrop. On October 29, the U.S. central bank lowered its benchmark federal funds rate by 0.25 percentage points at its second consecutive meeting, as it looked to balance a recent slowdown in hiring and inflation running higher than its 2% target. The S&P 500® rose 2.34% for the month, with the Fed pushing back against market expectations for a further rate cut in 2025.

Momentum faded a bit in November (+0.25%), as stocks weathered the shutdown and a brief mid-month sell-off that mostly reflected concern about a potential AI bubble. However, the focus returned to a U.S. economy largely driven by massive AI-related outlays among big tech firms. Meanwhile, the Fed cut rates by another quarter point on December 10. The index achieved a record close on Christmas Eve before a sluggish final week resulted in a flattish advance for the month, a 17.88% gain for the year and a 38.65% increase since the April 8 market low.

In the fourth quarter, the index's narrow advance was driven by the defensively-oriented health care sector (+12%), which particularly shined in November. The growth-leaning communication services sector rose about 7%, benefiting from the transformative potential of generative AI, as media & entertainment stocks rose 9%. All other groups lagged the index. Real estate (-3%) and utilities (-1%) – both small components of the S&P 500 – lost ground, while consumer staples finished at roughly breakeven. Materials, industrials and consumer discretionary stocks all gained roughly 1%, as did information technology, which represented 35% of the index in Q4. Financials (+2%) stocks reflected a strong showing by banks (+7%) but exhibited weakness (-1%) among insurers. Energy (+2%) was held back by a sharp decline in the price of oil.

Within this environment, the Fidelity Strategic Dividend & Income Composite Index SM was up 0.67% for the final three months of 2025. The index consists of four asset classes: high-dividend-yielding stocks, preferred stocks, real estate investment trusts and convertible securities. Dividend-paying equities, as measured by the MSCI USA High Dividend Yield Index, advanced 1.30% this quarter, propelled by the information technology, health care and energy sectors. In contrast, consumer discretionary, communication services and materials stocks fared the worst, finishing Q4 in the red. Meanwhile, REITs, according to the FTSE NAREIT Equity REITs Index, returned -1.56%. Most categories lost ground during the quarter, with office REITs performing especially poorly, whereas industrial REITs, however, had a strong showing. Elsewhere, convertible securities – hybrid investments that combine the features of both stocks and bonds – gained 1.97% this period, per the ICE BofA All U.S. Convertibles Index, whereas preferred stocks, as measured by the ICE BofA®Fixed Rate Preferred Securities index, returned -0.31%.

Outlook and Positioning

The fund employs a lead portfolio-manager/subportfolio-manager structure. The two co-lead portfolio managers make allocation decisions across the fund's major asset classes. Individual picks in each are made by experienced and specialized managers within their respective areas of expertise. Our asset allocation investment process begins with strategic weightings across the fund's asset classes. Depending on the co-lead portfolio managers' outlook for each type of investment, they may over- or underweight an asset class versus the neutral mix. This is done judiciously, with an eye toward long-term trends and in a strategic manner that aims to help preserve the integrity of the portfolio. Beyond that, each subportfolio has its own dedicated manager who employs an asset class-specific process for security selection. This process is designed to provide a well-diversified strategy with the potential to generate income regardless of the interest-rate environment. Based on Fidelity research, we have established a target mix of 50% dividend-yielding stocks, 20% preferred stocks, 15% convertible securities and 15% REITs. As the fund's neutral positioning, this is the combination of asset classes and weightings that, over time, we believe should provide the most favorable risk/reward for our shareholders. Our approach to managing the portfolio always is highly tactical, meaning we make shifts to the asset mix based on where we see opportunities in the marketplace at any given time.

The fund's investable universe also includes non-Composite asset classes – including master limited partnerships and infrastructure stocks – both of which provide additional opportunities to broaden the portfolio's dividend-paying-equity exposure. The out-of-index allocation to infrastructure securities averaged about 2% for the fourth quarter, while we also maintained a small non-index stake in MLPs totaling roughly 0.5% of net assets, on average, the past three months. In Q4, we continued to reallocate the fund's risk exposures. For example, we halved our overweight in infrastructure equities, given other opportunities we identified within our investment universe, coupled with the recent significant outperformance of this asset class.

With these proceeds, we added to REITs, bringing the fund's relative exposure up to neutral, while also increasing our existing overweight positioning in dividend-paying equities. The shift in REITs reflected investors' apparently deep pessimism about the asset class, given its weak performance of late. Still, we remained unconvinced about the valuation opportunity relative to other segments of the market, so we remained neutrally positioned here. Meanwhile, we upped the portfolio's allocation to equities – historically a main driver of the fund's relative result – because we see a potentially favorable stock picking environment. In short, we would characterize the fund as overweight risk, which is further reflected in our continued large underweight in preferred securities.

As of year-end, we are in "wait-and-see" mode, as various uncertainties weigh on investor sentiment. For example, market participants are wondering about where inflation and employment may be headed, and what that implies for further Fed interest rate cuts. Also, because many asset classes are seeing valuations at or near their historical highs, good news may already be fully priced into certain securities, as we see it. Against this backdrop, we are picking our spots when determining where to invest. As always, we will not hesitate to adjust the portfolio's allocations as conditions evolve, based on where we find value in the marketplace.■
